description: Work to restore a relationship to workable accord after conflict, estrangement, or harm. Reconciliation may acknowledge what happened and make room for repair without returning the relationship to its former shape. 'tholira' (forgive) can be one person's release of grievance; 'warosha' concerns the relation among participants.
articulatory_notes: Round the lips and raise the back of the tongue for voiced 'w', then open into central 'a'. Tap or briefly trill the tongue tip for stressed 'r' and move into middle-height back rounded 'o'. Raise the tongue blade towards the postalveolar region for unvoiced 'sh', direct air through the narrow channel, and finish on open central 'a'.
sound_symbolism: The form begins with an approaching glide, turns on the stressed tap, and ends in a broad hush. That movement offers a mnemonic for participants coming back into relation while the trace of conflict remains audible.
usage_notes: Participants may take reciprocal 'wiso' or a comitative phrase with 'nua'. With reflexive 'miso', a person works to bring conflicting parts of their own life or account into workable relation.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: A reconciled relation need not recover its old shape. Change can be acknowledged without fixing either participant permanently to the conflict.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: Reconciliation has its own verb because peace after harm requires work on the relationship, not only an end to open conflict or one person's forgiveness.

semantic_domains.community: work on a relationship after conflict, estrangement, or harm
semantic_domains.ethics: reconciliation pursued alongside acknowledgement and repair
